---
title: "Our coding + genAI toolkit: intro and setup"
subtitle: "An introduction to, and setup of, the main tools we'll use in the workshop"
number-sections: true
date: 2026-07-03
date-modified: today
author:
  - name: Jelmer Poelstra
    orcid: 0000-0002-3514-7462
    email: poelstra.1@osu.edu
    affiliation: 
      - name: CFAES Bioinformatics Core, Ohio State University
        url: https://mcic.osu.edu/bioinformatics
other-links:
  - text: "Next up: Using GitHub Copilot and Claude Code"
    icon: bookmarks
    href: 07-agentic1.html
---

---------

<hr style="height:1pt; visibility:hidden;" />

::: {.callout-caution appearance="simple"}
**_This page is still subject to change_**
:::

<hr style="height:1pt; visibility:hidden;" />

## Introduction

In the rest of this workshop, we'll primarily use the following set of tools to
run code, access data, and make use of agentic AI tools:

1. A code editor, **VS Code**

2. A shared computing environment, **OSC** (the Ohio Supercomputer Center)

3. Generative and Agentic AI tools, **GitHub Copilot** and **Claude Code**, in the form of plugins for VS Code

In this session, we will go through the necessary steps be able to use this setup,
providing context about the various tools and their use in and after the workshop.

::: {.callout-tip appearance="simple"}
This same setup is what I use in my own work,
and you can continue use it after the workshop
(with some caveats regarding the AI tools, which we'll discuss).
Getting this setup configured on your own computer is a bit of a process,
but it is well worth it in my opinion.
:::

## Exploring VS Code

### Introduction

VS Code is basically a **fancy text editor**.
To emphasize the additional functionality relative to basic text editors like Notepad,
editors like VS Code are also referred to as **IDEs**: Integrated Development Environments^[
The RStudio program is another good example of an IDE ---
and like RStudio is an IDE for R, VS Code will be our IDE for shell (and other) code.].

::: callout-note
#### Why we use VS Code here
It's a great editor all around, and when working at OSC in particular
---at least if you need to run command-line programs, and not just, say, R---
it's by far the most convenient and user-friendly way, in my opinion.

You can also use VS Code at OSC in a web browser via OSC OnDemand,
like we did in the pre-workshop session.
That's nice because it requires no installation or other setup.

But in the long run, connecting your own VS Code installation to OSC is preferable.
In the context of this workshop, for example:
we can't use AI tools like GitHub Copilot in the OnDemand version of VS Code,
but we can when connecting our own VS Code installation to OSC.
:::

You should have already installed VS Code (full name: Visual Studio Code) on your laptop.
Go ahead and open it.

![Finding VS Code with a Mac's Spotlight Search.](/img/vscode-open.png){fig-align="center" width="75%" fig-alt="Screenshot of the VS Code icon on a Mac"}

### The VS Code User Interface

![A screenshot of the VS Code user interface [from the official VS Code docs](https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/editing/userinterface). In this example, multiple files are open in the editor pane (Editor Groups), and those have been split into two side-by-side groups.](/img/vscode-ui.png){fig-align="center" width="100%" fig-alt="An annotated screenshot of the VS Code user interace" .lightbox}

- The main part of the VS Code window is the **Editor Pane** (`C` above),
  where you can open and edit text files.

- The panel at the bottom (`D` above) contains a **terminal**, but isn't open by default.<br>
  {{< fa user-edit >}} In the top menu, click `Terminal` > `New Terminal` to open it.

- By default, the **Primary Side Bar** (`B` above) shows a file explorer,
  where you can see your files and create new ones, and so on.

- The **Activity Bar** (narrow side bar, `A` above) on the far left has:
  
  - Icons to toggle between options for the Primary Side Bar.
    For now, pay attention to the bottom one, Extensions, which we'll use soon.
  
  - A {{< fa cog >}} (cog wheel icon) in the bottom, with two key options:
    - **Command Palette**, where you can access all VS Code functionality
    - **Settings**, where you can configure VS Code 

::: exercise
#### {{< fa user-edit >}} Exercises

1. In the cog wheel {{< fa cog >}} menu, click `Themes` > `Color Theme`
   to explore VS Code color themes and optionally **change the color theme** to your liking.

2. You can **resize panes** by dragging the borders between them:
   try doing this with the Primary Side Bar and the Panel containing the terminal.

3. If you didn't do the pre-workshop learning,
   you won't yet have **your own dir** `/fs/scratch/PAS3454/people/<user>`.
   Create this dir, where `<user>` should be your exact OSC username.

   <details><summary>Click to see some hints</summary>

   You can create this dir in several ways, but here is the most bulletproof one:

   ```bash
   # `$USER` is an environment variable that contains your username,
   # so this exact command will work for everyone:
   mkdir /fs/scratch/PAS3454/people/$USER
   ```
   
   Alternatively, assuming you are in `/fs/scratch/PAS3454` and your username is
   `jane`, a simpler command would be:

   ```bash
   mkdir people/jane
   ```

   ::: {.callout-tip appearance="simple"}
   If you already had a `/fs/scratch/PAS3454/people/<user>` dir,
   the above commands should fail with an error message like
   `mkdir: cannot create directory ‘/fs/scratch/PAS3454/people/jane’: File exists`.
   :::

   Other alternatives include using the VS Code file explorer (there's a "New Folder" button)
   or the [OSC OnDemand](https://ondemand.osc.edu/) file browser.

   </details>
:::

## Setting up the OSC connection in VS Code

### Configure the connection to OSC

Now, we'll do some **one-time set up** to enable connecting VS Code to OSC via so-called "SSH tunneling".
This process moves a VS Code window _entirely_ to OSC:
the terminal, the file system, the code editor, and so on^[
In case you're familiar with the `ssh` command, this is similar to connecting your terminal to OSC with `ssh <username>@cardinal.osc.edu`.
But instead of connecting just your terminal, this approach allows you to run the entire VS Code environment on OSC,
which has many benefits.
].

Once configured, this is a very convenient and powerful way to access OSC.

#### Install the Remote Development extension

1. In the VS Code Activity Bar, click the **Extensions** icon at the bottom (left screenshot below).

:::: colummns
::: {.column width="43%"}
![Screenshot of the VS Code Activity Bar, with the Extensions icon at the bottom. *[TBA - highlight icon]*](/img/vscode-narrow-sidebar-items.png){fig-align="center" width="14%" fig-alt="Screenshot of the VS Code Activity Bar, showing the Extensions icon highlighted"}
:::

::: {.column width="51%"}
![Screenshot showing the Remove Development extension popping up when you search for it. Clicking the "Install" button will install the extension.](img/vscode-remote-dev.png){fig-align="center" width="90%" fig-alt="Screenshot of the VS Code Remote Development extension"}
:::
::::

2. Now, the Primary (wide) Sidebar will show the Extensions view, where you can search for and install extensions.
   In the search box, type "_remote dev_" and the 
   "[Remote Development](https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=ms-vscode-remote.vscode-remote-extensionpack)"
   extension should appear (right screenshot above).

4. Click the "Install" button to install this extension.

#### Setup the connection

Next, we'll use functionality from this extension pack to set up the connection to OSC:

4. Open the VS Code **Command Palette**^[
   cog wheel {{< fa cog >}} => Command Palette or press <kbd>Ctrl/⌘</kbd>+<kbd>Shift</kbd>+<kbd>P</kbd>]
   and start typing "_remote open_".

5. Click the top option that shows up, "**Remote-SSH: Open SSH Configuration File...**",
   and then again the top option, a file that contains your computer username and ends in `/.ssh/config`
   (see screenshots below):

:::: colummns
::: {.column width="48%"}
![After typing remote open in the Command Palette, select the option shown in this screenshot (should be the only one to appear).](/img/vscode-open-ssh-config.png){fig-align="center" width="98%" fig-alt="Screenshot of the VS Code Command Palette, showing the 'Remote-SSH: Open SSH Configuration File...' option highlighted"}
:::
::: {.column width="3%"}
:::
::: {.column width="48%"}
![Next, a few options will appear. Select the top one, which should contain the username you have on your computer.](/img/vscode-open-ssh-config2.png){fig-align="center" width="85%" fig-alt="Screenshot of the VS Code Command Palette, showing the 'Remote-SSH: Open SSH Configuration File...' option highlighted"}
:::
::::

6. Paste the following (_note Mac vs. Windows instructions_) into the file that
   opens, then:
   
   - In two places, change `User <osc-username>` to your OSC username
     (for example, to `User jane`).
   
   - Save the file (`File` > `Save` or <kbd>Ctrl/⌘</kbd>+<kbd>S</kbd>).

   ::: {.panel-tabset}
   
   ### Windows
   
   ```markdown
   Host osc-cardinal
       User <osc-username>
       HostName cardinal.osc.edu

   Host osc-cardinal-compute
       User <osc-username>
       ForwardAgent yes
       StrictHostKeyChecking no
       UserKnownHostsFile=/dev/null
       ProxyCommand ssh osc-cardinal "/usr/bin/salloc -A PAS3454 -t 300 /bin/bash -c 'nc $SLURM_NODELIST 22'"
   ```

   ### Mac
   
   ```markdown
   Host osc-cardinal
       User <osc-username>
       HostName cardinal.osc.edu

   Host osc-cardinal-compute
       User <osc-username>
       ForwardAgent yes
       StrictHostKeyChecking no
       UserKnownHostsFile=/dev/null
       ProxyCommand ssh osc-cardinal "/usr/bin/salloc -A PAS3454 -t 300 /bin/bash -c 'nc \$SLURM_NODELIST 22'"
   ```
   :::

7. Open the VS Code **Settings** (cog wheel {{< fa cog >}} => Settings) and search for `remote.SSH.connectTimeout`.
   Change the value from 15 to 300 (seconds).

![Find the connection timeout setting and change it to 300 seconds, as shown in this screenshot.](/img/vscode-connection-timeout.png){fig-align="center" width="95%" fig-alt="Screenshot of the VS Code Settings, showing the 'Remote.SSH: Connect Timeout' option highlighted"}

::: callout-note
#### What we just did

In this SSH configuration file, we defined two remote "hosts" that we can connect to:

- `osc-cardinal`, which will connect to a **login node** of OSC's Cardinal cluster.
  However, because the VS Code program will actually run on the login node when
  you connect it with SSH-tunneling, the below connection to a compute node is preferred.

- `osc-cardinal-compute` will connect to a **compute node** of OSC's Cardinal cluster.
  We can't connect directly to a compute node but have to request a compute job,
  which is why that connection is more complicated.
  This connection will last for 5 hours (300 minutes),
  as it requests a compute job of that duration.

Because compute jobs (for `osc-cardinal-compute`) may take a while to start,
we also increased the "connection timeout"
(amount of time VS Code will wait for the connection to be established) to 5 minutes.
:::

::: {.callout-tip}
#### Connecting to other OSC clusters
To configure connect to other OSC clusters (currently, Pitzer and Ascend),
you can add additional entries to this SSH configuration file,
simply replacing `cardinal` by the name of the other cluster._
:::

::: {.callout-warning collapse="true"}
#### For after the workshop: Changing the account to be charged for compute time _(Click to expand)_
The `-A PAS3454` part of the connection instructs OSC to charge the compute time
to the workshop's OSC Project.
While it's OK to keep using this for a bit after the workshop ---
if you have access to another OSC project, you should change `PAS3454` to that project's number.
:::

### Connect to OSC

With the one-time configuration steps done, you can from now on connect to OSC like so:

1. Open the Command Palette and start typing "_**SSH connect**_"

2. Click on `SSH: Connect to Host` and then select the second of the connections
   you just set up, `osc-cardinal-compute`:

![](/img/vscode-select-connection.png){fig-align="center" width="60%" fig-alt="Screenshot of the VS Code Command Palette, showing the 'SSH: Connect to Host' option highlighted"}

3. A new VS Code window will open. In the little pop-up at the top, enter your OSC password
   _(this won't be necessary in the future if you do the optional setup below)_.

4. The connection may take a little while (typically 5–30 seconds, could be longer if you're unlucky)
   to establish, because a compute job has to be requested, assigned resources, and started.
   
   Once connected, a pop-up may appear, asking whether you "trust the authors"
   of the folder you ended up in: click `Trust and Continue`.

   ![](/img/vscode-trust.png){fig-align="center" width="60%" fig-alt="Screenshot of the VS Code pop-up asking whether to trust the authors of the folder you ended up in, with the 'Trust and Continue' button highlighted"}

   ::: {.callout-important appearance="simple"}
   If the connection fails or takes more than a minute to establish, ask us for troubleshooting help.
   As a last resort, try connecting to `osc-cardinal` (the login node connection) instead. 
   :::

### Open a folder on OSC

VS Code takes a specific
**folder as a starting point in all parts of the program**:
in its file explorer, in the terminal, when saving files, and so on.

By default, this folder is your Home directory.
But in this workshop, we'll only work in a folder specific to the workshop,
so let's open that one now.

{{< fa user-edit >}} Click `File` > `Open Folder` and then enter and select `/fs/scratch/PAS3454`.

This will cause the program to reload and the "trust popup" will appear again^[
for this folder, it won't appear again in the future, once you've trusted it.].

In the File Explorer, you should now see files ... TBA ....

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
#### From now on: Connecting directly to a specific folder at OSC _(Click to expand)_

The above two-step procedure (connect to OSC, then open a folder) can be done
in one even simpler step, after you've connected to that folder at least once:

Once you re-open VS Code (or open a new window with `File` > `New Window`),
the `Recent` section in the VS Code "Welcome" document will now show the folder(s)
you previously opened:

![](/img/vscode-recents.png){fig-align="center" width="40%" fig-alt="Screenshot of the VS Code Welcome document, showing the 'Recents' section with the 'Open Folder...' button highlighted"}

Simply clicking on that line will open that folder and in the process connect to OSC!
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}

### Optional setup so you won't have to enter your OSC password again _(Click to expand)_

If you take the following steps,
you will no longer be prompted for your OSC password when connecting to OSC via SSH,
including when connecting VS Code to OSC via the SSH tunneling we just set up.

1. Open the terminal in VS Code (in a window that's on your own computer, **not** at OSC).

2. In the terminal (regardless of whether you're using Windows or Mac),
   execute the following command to generate a public-private SSH key-pair:

    ``` {.bash}
    ssh-keygen -t rsa
    ```

3. You'll get some (odd-looking!) output and will then be asked several questions.
   In each case, you can **simply press <kbd>Enter</kbd> to select the default answer**;
   do this until you get your command prompt back.

3. Excute the following command to transfer the public key to OSC's Cardinal cluster,
   **but make sure to replace `<user>` with your OSC username**:

   ::: {.panel-tabset}
   ### Windows
  
   ```bash
   # Replace <user> by your username, e.g. "jelmer@cardinal.osc.edu"
   cat ~/.ssh/id_rsa.pub | ssh <user>@cardinal.osc.edu 'mkdir -p .ssh && cat >> .ssh/authorized_keys'
   ```
  
   ### Mac
   ```bash
   # Replace <user> by your username, e.g. "jelmer@cardinal.osc.edu"
   ssh-copy-id <user>@cardinal.osc.edu
   ```
   :::

4. All done! Test if it works by connecting to OSC once again.

:::

## Setting up GitHub Copilot

### Background

GitHub Copilot is an generative and agentic AI tool that can help you write code
and perform other tasks.
It's the first of two similar tools we will use in this workshop, the other being Claude Code.

GitHub Copilot is **not** the same as the "general" Microsoft Copilot.
And while GitHub Copilot _is_ a Microsoft product, hence the similar name,
you cannot –currently– use it institutionally at OSU.
Instead, access runs through a **personal GitHub account**.
It's easy and free to create a GitHub account,
and you may already have one from previous usage or because you followed 
[this optional pre-workshop setup step](s3-github-account.qmd).

Keep in mind that GitHub is a much more general platform than just GitHub Copilot,
widely used for sharing and collaborating on code.
Nevertheless, with your GitHub account,
you are nowadays also automatically enrolled in the GitHub Copilot free plan.

::: {.callout-note}
#### GitHub Copilot plans

The GitHub Copilot free plan will work for the purposes of this workshop,
but is _not_ sufficient for sustained Copilot use.

You may have done the optional pre-workshop setup step to 
[obtain GitHub Copilot Student/Pro access via your academic credentials](s3-github-account.qmd).
If that was approved (should be quick) and processed (72 hours later),
you'll now have more extensive, though not unlimited, access to GitHub Copilot.
For more details, see [our GitHub setup page](s3-github-account.qmd#apply-for-additional-free-github-copilot-access)

If you didn't do that, there isn't much point in doing it _right now_,
because you won't obtain access during the workshop given the 72-hour processing time.
But feel free to do so in a break or after the workshop.
:::

While there's also a stand-alone app and web-based interface for it,
**we will use GitHub Copilot within VS Code**, where it is available as a pre-installed plugin.
Therefore, little setup is needed:
the steps below only serve to log in to your GitHub account or create one along the way.

### The setup steps

1. Towards the right side of the top VS Code bar, click the **Sign In** button to log into GitHub:

   ![](/img/vscode-copilot-signin.png){fig-align="center" width="65%" fig-alt="Screenshot of the VS Code interface, showing the GitHub Copilot icon in the top right corner of the window"}

   ::: {.callout-warning collapse="true"}
   ### Don't see the Sign In button? _(Click to expand)_

   In the VS Code Status Bar (the thin bar way at the bottom),
   there should be a **GitHub Copilot** icon ---
   the icon to the left of the bell in the screenshot below:

   ![](/img/vscode-copilot-icon.png){fig-align="center" width="20%" fig-alt="Screenshot of the VS Code interface, showing the GitHub Copilot icon in the top right corner of the window"}

   Your version of the icon may be striked-through, which means that GitHub Copilot is not yet active.
   Click on the icon and the login pop-up should appear.
   :::

3. A pop-up similar to the left-hand image below should appear,
   and if you click on Continue with GitHub, you should see something like the right-hand image:

   ![Logging into GitHub to use GitHub Copilot. [From the GitHub Blog](https://github.blog/changelog/2025-07-15-social-login-with-google-is-now-generally-available).](img/github-login-vscode.png){fig-align="center" width="80%"}

   ::: callout-tip
   #### GitHub account options

   - For those **without** a GitHub account, you can create one on the fly, either with
     the "Create an account" button on the bottom, or semi-automatically via an existing
     Google login by clicking the "Continue with Google" button.

   - For those **with** a GitHub account, you can log in with your existing credentials.
     Don't use Continue with Google unless you know your GitHub account is already linked to your Google account,
     or account confusion may arise.
   :::

   ::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
   ### Were you already signed in to GitHub? Then the prompt should look like this _(Click to expand)_
   
   ![](/img/copilot-already-signed-in.png){fig-align="center" width="55%" fig-alt="Screenshot of the VS Code interface, showing the GitHub Copilot login prompt"}
   :::

4. Finally, you may be prompted to authorize VS Code to access your GitHub account, which you should do:

![](/img/copilot-signin-send-back.png){fig-align="center" width="45%" fig-alt="Screenshot of the GitHub login page, showing the 'Authorize Visual Studio Code' button highlighted"}

5. GitHub Copilot lives in a "Secondary Side Bar" on the righthand side,
   which you can toggle by pressing
   the rightmost icon in the screenshot below, on the right side of the top VS Code bar:

   ![Clicking the rightmost icon toggles the Secondary Side Bar with GitHub Copilot. _TBA: highlight icon_](/img/vscode-copilot-sidebar.png){fig-align="center" width="35%" fig-alt="Screenshot of the VS Code interface, showing the GitHub Copilot sidebar"}

   That AI chat sidebar should look something like this:

   ![](/img/vscode-copilot-blank.png){fig-align="center" width="50%" fig-alt="Screenshot of the VS Code interface, showing the GitHub Copilot sidebar"}

::: {.callout-tip appearance="simple"}
As mentioned above, unless you have already [obtained GitHub Copilot Student/Pro access via your academic credentials](s3-github-account.qmd),
you will be automatically using the GitHub Copilot free plan after logging in or creating a new account.
:::

### One example prompt

Let's give it one quick try, before we go into more detail in the next sessions.
In the Copilot prompt box
(which should have the ghost text "_Describe what to build_"), enter:

> _How many reads are in data/iqbal/SRR31869590_1.fastq?_

Because we're asking Copilot for information it can only get after actually doing
something (running a command), it will by default ask for permission to do so:

![](/img/copilot-asks-permission.png){fig-align="center" width="70%" fig-alt="Screenshot of the GitHub Copilot prompt asking for permission to run a command"}

Click `Allow` and it will run the command and return the answer:

![TBA - Replace with subsampled FASTQ files](/img/copilot-prompt1-answer.png){fig-align="center" width="70%" fig-alt="Screenshot of the GitHub Copilot prompt showing the answer to the question about how many reads are in the specified fastq file"}

::: callout-note
#### What did we just witness --- and some context
This example shows how in-editor AI like GitHub Copilot can perform actions
themselves, rather than just providing information or code snippets.
In technical terms, as part of this agentic behavior, it "calls a tool"
(or: "performs **tool calls**") that allow it to run arbitrary commands in the terminal.

We'll soon see another type of extremely useful in-editor agentic behavior,
where it can **read and/or edit your code**.
This latter type of behavior is what we'll use most in this workshop.
That's because broadly speaking, the example above notwithstanding,
_the goal in this workshop is not to let AI analyze the data for us,_
_but rather to let it help us write code to appropriately analyze the data with_
_existing non-AI programs._
:::

## Setting up Claude Code

### Background

**Like with GitHub Copilot**, the Anthropic product Claude Code comes in various forms,
but we will use it within VS Code, where it is available as a plugin.

**Unlike with GitHub Copilot**, we will not use a personal account to access Claude Code.
Instead, we will use an access key that the workshop instructors have obtained
using OSU OTDI's [Generative AI Service](https://it.osu.edu/generative-ai-service).

The advantages of this approach ---not just during this workshop, but for you moving forward--- are:

1. It is approved at OSU for use with institutional data,
   all the way to the most sensitive category (S4 classification). <span style="font-size: 1.3em;">🎉</span>
2. At least in principle, you can get a work-related rather than personal funding source for your AI usage.
3. As it happens, OSU just started an initiative where anyone at OSU can get $100 worth of AI credits
   using the exact same mechanism that we will use in this workshop. <span style="font-size: 1.3em;">🥳</span>

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
#### Wait, why are we showing GitHub Copilot at all, again? _(Click to expand)_

- For academics, there is a generous (more so for teachers than students)
  free personal option for GitHub Copilot, which is not the case for Claude Code.

- After using your abovementioned credits via the [Generative AI Service](https://it.osu.edu/generative-ai-service)
  you may not be able to keep using this  because you don't have a work-related funding source to pay for it.

- Even though it's ideal to use a tool approved for private data,
  you may not be working with private data at all, or be able to get help without exposing it.
  If so, then GitHub Copilot is a perfectly fine option.

- Seemingly minor but very handy: only GitHub Copilot has inline "code completion" features
  (_And:_ if you only use these, any data privacy concerns can be circumvented.)
:::

::: {.callout-warning collapse="true"}
#### Don't get confused about data approval contexts _(Click to expand)_
Claude Code is **only** approved for use with institutional data when it is accessed
via an access key (API key) from OTDI's Generative AI Service.
If you pay for a personal Anthropic account and use that to access Claude Code,
you are **not** using it in an approved way. 
:::

### The setup steps

1. In the **Extensions** view in the sidebar, search for "claude" and install the extension
   "[Claude Code for VS Code](https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=anthropic.claude-code)":

![](/img/vscode-claude-install.png){fig-align="center" width="50%" fig-alt="Screenshot of the VS Code Extensions sidebar, showing the Claude Code extension highlighted"}

2. To authenticate to Claude Code, we will use a settings file with an access key
   called an API key.
   **In the terminal**, copy this file to your Home directory
   (where it will be automatically read by Claude Code):

   ```bash
   cp /fs/scratch/PAS3454/share/settings.json ~/.claude/settings.json
   ```

   ::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
   #### What does this file contain? _(Click to expand)_

   We can't share this file on the website in full,
   because it contains an API key that cannot be exposed publicly.
   But for your reference, the contents of this file
   minus the API key (here replaced by `REPLACE_WITH_API_KEY`) are as follows:
   
   ```json
   {
   "theme": "auto",
   "env": {                                                                                                                                                                                        
       "ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL": "https://litellm.cloud.osu.edu",                                                                                                                                        
       "ANTHROPIC_AUTH_TOKEN": <REPLACE_WITH_API_KEY>,
       "ANTHROPIC_DEFAULT_OPUS_MODEL": "claude-opus-4-6",
       "ANTHROPIC_DEFAULT_SONNET_MODEL": "claude-sonnet-4-6",
       "ANTHROPIC_DEFAULT_HAIKU_MODEL": "claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
       "CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_EXPERIMENTAL_BETAS": "1"                                                                                                                                        
       }   
   }
   ```
   :::

3. A second tab should now be available in the side bar with Copilot,
   named "CLAUDE" --- click on that
   (_if you don't see it, you may have to widen the side bar!_)::

   ![Next to "CHAT" (i.e., GitHub Copilot), a "CLAUDE CODE" tab is now available.](/img/vscode-claude-in-sidebar.png){fig-align="center" width="70%" fig-alt="Screenshot of the VS Code interface, showing the Claude Code icon in the top right corner of the window"}

   ::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
   #### Alternative way of opening a Claude Code window _(Click to expand)_
   
   Once installed, a Claude Code icon will appear in the topright of the editor pane:
   
   ![](/img/vscode-claude-icon.png){fig-align="center" width="20%" fig-alt="Screenshot of the VS Code interface, showing the Claude Code icon in the top right corner of the window"}
   
   Clicking this icon will also open a Claude Code window,
   but this one will be opened within the editor pane rather than in the sidebar.
   :::

4. You should for some moments see the following messages in the Claude Code window,
   indicating that you are not authenticated yet:
   
   ![](/img/claude-login.png){fig-align="center" width="50%" fig-alt="Screenshot of the Claude Code interface, showing the first window after logging in, with a message indicating that you are not authenticated yet"}

   Simply wait, and if all goes well, Claude will process the settings file behind
   the scenes, and switch to an interface similar to the Copilot one:

   ![](/img/claude-first-window.png){fig-align="center" width="55%" fig-alt="Screenshot of the Claude Code interface, showing the first window after logging in"}

   ::: {.callout-important}
   #### Does the window with login options not automatically disappear?
   Don't try to log in via the options in that window,
   but warn the instructors and we'll troubleshoot.
   :::

5. In the prompt box at the bottom,
   go ahead and ask it a quick, simple question to test that it works.

   ::: {.callout-tip appearance="simple"}
   No inspiration? Ask a classic stumbling-block question for AIs like:

   > How many "b"s are in the word "blueberry"?
   :::

<hr style="height:1pt; visibility:hidden;" />

## Appendix

### Switching to your own API key

TBA

<https://go.osu.edu/api-fluency>

### VS Code settings

Open the VS Code **Settings** (cog wheel {{< fa cog >}} > `Settings`) and search
for the following settings to change them:

- Turn off "Sticky Scroll" in the terminal, which is a newish feature that as far
  as I can tell seems to malfunction and can be really annoying,
  as it will e.g. hide the line you just executed. 

![](/img/vscode-setting-sticky-scroll.png){fig-align="center" width="100%" fig-alt="Screenshot of the VS Code Settings, showing the 'Terminal > Integrated: Sticky Scroll' option highlighted"}

- More TBA

::: {.callout-warning collapse="true"}
#### Auto Save in VS Code _(Click to expand)_

Any changes you make to this and other files in the editor pane should be
immediately, automatically saved by VS Code.
If that's not happening for some reason, you should see an indication of unsaved
changes like a large black dot next to the script's file name in the editor pane tab header.

If the file is not auto-saving, you can always save it manually
(including with <kbd>Ctrl/Cmd</kbd>+<kbd>S</kbd>) like you would do in other programs.

However, it may be convenient to turn Auto Save on:
press <kbd>Ctrl/Cmd</kbd>+<kbd>Shift</kbd>+<kbd>S</kbd> to open the Command Palette and
type "Auto Save". You should see an option "**_Toggle Auto Save_**": click on that.
:::
